context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: State

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What is the role of the State in development planning? Is the 'Third World State' expected to behave differently from the 'First World' State in its relation to industry, private sector, financial markets and social programs? What is the impact of globalization on the State? What distinguishes a State from other actors in planning?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m trying to write my reflection on the role of the state in development planning, and I’m kind of stuck on how to organize it. I get that the prompt wants me to compare “Third World” and “First World” states and talk about globalization, but I’m not sure what my main argument should be.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point. Before thinking about structure, let's focus on substance. What do YOU think is the most fundamental difference between how a 'Third World' state and a 'First World' state approaches development? Start there — your argument will likely grow from your answer to that question.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the process and asking for organizational help, which is a reasonable starting point. Rather than giving them a structure or thesis, I should use the Socratic method to help them discover their own argument by asking them to reflect on what they already think about the core question. This keeps the intellectual work with the student.

exchanges.student: I think maybe the biggest difference is capacity? Like, a “First World” state usually has stronger institutions and more resources, so it can regulate markets and provide social programs more consistently. A “Third World” state seems like it has to do development while also dealing with weaker institutions, external pressure, and sometimes basic state-building at the same time.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point. But think about this: if it's just a matter of capacity, then the solution would simply be to 'build capacity' and Third World states would eventually behave like First World states. Do you think that's the whole story, or is there something more fundamental shaping how Third World states operate?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a solid initial observation about capacity and institutional strength. This is a good foundation but it's somewhat surface-level. I should push them to think deeper — specifically about what makes this difference meaningful for development planning, and whether capacity alone fully explains the distinction. I want to guide them toward thinking about the unique pressures on Third World states (colonialism, external actors, globalization) without giving those ideas to them directly.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I don’t think it’s only capacity. I think history matters too — like colonial legacies, dependence on foreign capital or aid, and weaker political legitimacy maybe shape what these states can even do. So it’s not just “they have less ability,” but also that they operate in a different global and historical context.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a much stronger observation. Now connect it to the prompt: given this different historical and structural context, how does globalization specifically change what Third World states can do? Does it give them more room to maneuver, or less?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making real progress, connecting capacity to historical and structural factors. This is getting closer to a meaningful argument. Now I should push them to connect this insight to the specific questions in the prompt — particularly about globalization and what distinguishes the state from other actors. I don't want to dwell too long on this point before moving them forward.

exchanges.student: I’m leaning toward “both,” but mostly less. Globalization can bring investment, trade, and access to ideas or technology, but it also seems to limit state autonomy because governments have to respond to global markets, donors, and institutions, not just domestic needs. I’m not fully sure how to say that without sounding too vague though.
exchanges.tutor: You're on the right track. To avoid vagueness, think concretely: can you name a specific type of external actor or institution that constrains Third World state autonomy? What does that constraint actually look like in practice?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a nuanced position on globalization but is worried about vagueness. Rather than helping them phrase it (which would violate the rule about not editing or suggesting language), I should push them to ground their claim in a concrete mechanism or example that they can draw on. This will naturally sharpen their argument without me doing the writing for them.

exchanges.student: Maybe the IMF or World Bank? I think the constraint looks like policy conditions attached to loans, so a state might have to cut public spending, privatize, or liberalize trade even if that conflicts with its own development priorities. I’m a little unsure if that’s too simplistic, but that’s the example that comes to mind first.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid, concrete example — not too simplistic at all. Now there's one part of the prompt you haven't touched yet: what distinguishes the state from other actors in planning? The IMF is also a powerful actor — so what does the state have that the IMF, an NGO, or a private firm doesn't?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a strong, concrete example (IMF/World Bank conditionality) that directly supports their argument. I should validate this without over-praising, and now push them toward the last major piece of the prompt they haven't addressed yet: what distinguishes the state from other actors in planning. This will help them build toward a complete reflection.

exchanges.student: I think the state is different because it has formal sovereignty and legitimate authority over a territory, at least in theory. It can make and enforce laws, collect taxes, allocate public resources, and set the institutional rules that other actors have to operate within. So even if NGOs, firms, or donors influence planning, the state is still kind of the main rule-maker.
exchanges.tutor: You've now developed solid thinking on all parts of the prompt. You have an argument about capacity and historical context, a concrete example about globalization constraining autonomy, and a clear sense of what makes the state unique. It's time to start writing. What would your opening argument — your central claim for the whole reflection — look like in your own words?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now covered all the major components of the prompt: the distinction between First/Third World states, the role of history and colonial legacies, the impact of globalization, and what distinguishes the state from other actors. This is a good moment to push them toward integrating all of this into a coherent argument for their reflection piece. They've been discussing ideas for several exchanges — it's time to move toward writing.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like: the state is the central actor in development planning because it has unique authority to make and enforce rules, but in the developing world its role is shaped by weaker institutional capacity, colonial legacies, and stronger pressure from global actors. So “Third World” states aren’t just weaker versions of “First World” states — they often have to plan under very different historical and political constraints.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid central claim — it's specific and avoids oversimplification. One thing to consider as you draft: your prompt also implicitly asks what the state *should* do given these constraints. Does your reflection take any position on that, or does it stay purely descriptive? That might be worth addressing as you write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a clear and substantive central claim that integrates the key themes they've developed. This is good progress. Rather than evaluating it extensively, I should encourage them to start drafting and flag one thing they might want to think about as they write — namely, whether their argument takes a clear enough position on the normative or prescriptive dimension (what SHOULD the state do, given these constraints?). This could strengthen the reflection without me doing the writing.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think I should take at least a small position. Maybe that the state still has to play a leading role in development planning, even under globalization, but it can’t act as if it’s fully autonomous and it probably has to negotiate with markets and external actors more strategically.
exchanges.tutor: Good — that gives your reflection a real argumentative edge. You have everything you need: a central claim, historical and structural context, a concrete example, a sense of what makes the state unique, and a normative position. Start drafting now. Come back when you have something written and we can work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed a normative position to complement their descriptive argument. They have all the ingredients for a strong reflection piece. This is a good moment to push them to actually start drafting rather than continuing to develop ideas in conversation — otherwise we risk going in circles. I should be direct about this.

exchanges.student: Okay, thanks — I think I can start now. I’ll try to draft around that claim and then come back if my paragraphs feel messy or too descriptive.
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. When you come back, share what you've written and we'll work through any issues together — but remember, the writing and revising is yours to do. Good luck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is ready to write and has a clear plan. A brief, encouraging send-off is appropriate here. I should remind them that when they return, I won't edit or polish their writing but will help them think through any issues they encounter.

exchanges.student: Got it — I’ll bring back my own draft once I have one. Thanks.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds good — see you when you have a draft.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is signing off to go write. A brief acknowledgment is appropriate. Nothing more is needed here.

grade.overview: The tutor conducted an exemplary Socratic dialogue across all ten turns. No submission-ready content was provided; the student generated all key ideas, claims, and examples. The tutor consistently asked probing follow-up questions, built on each student response, and guided the student toward a complete argument without doing the reasoning work for them. Responses were concise, well-targeted, and supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under any criterion. The tutor never gave a final answer or near-submission-ready content (1.1.A), never rewrote student text (1.1.B), and never stated the key idea before the student derived it (1.1.C). Scaffolding was excellent: the tutor acknowledged each student contribution and built progressively toward a full argument (1.2). Method-level feedback was present, e.g., prompting the student to move from vague claims to concrete examples (1.3). The dialogue advanced steadily without repetition (2.1), stayed anchored to the assignment throughout (2.2), used appropriately brief and clear responses (3.1), and maintained a warm, formative tone without implying grading authority (3.2).
